data.title: RXO Is Not Attractive Even Under Optimistic Assumptions On Return On Capital
data.description:
RXO, Inc. (RXO) is a truck freight broker, an 'asset-light' type of business exposed to the trucking cycle, but with different capital requirements (compared to other names I have recently reviewed).

The company is a recent (2022) spin-off from XPO, Inc. (XPO), and therefore its operating history is not very long, but the evidence we have (importantly from the 2020/22 cycle onwards) is not particularly positive in terms of returns on capital. I believe the reason is that RXO participates in a low-barrier-to-entry, low-return, highly commoditized business.

Having this in mind, I cannot understand how the company, which is currently generating operating losses and can cover interest only because of underinvestment, can trade at almost 20x average-cycle earnings or 10x peak-cycle earnings. I don't believe RXO is an opportunity, except for very speculative purposes, and therefore initiate the name as a Hold.

Business Introduction

RXO is a truck freight broker. The company's business is to connect shippers (companies that want to move loads) with carriers (owners and operators of truck fleets to move those loads). RXO may also consolidate billing and payments for shippers and carriers, provide and obtain volume discounts, etc. In general, the company makes a fairly regular gross margin on the dollar volume of freight brokered. In recent years, that number has fallen because of the deep trucking downturn, which squeezes margins across the industry.

In addition to brokerage (70%+ of revenues according to the latest 10-K), the company also does some managed transportation services (a little more white globe service that manages the logistics and freight needs of a client, for 10% of revenues) and last-mile (similar to brokerage, but for short-hauls, 20% of revenues). The company does not disclose these other two businesses as segments, and all is lumped together into a single operating segment on the 10-K, outside of revenues.

Critical Margin Situation

Because of the above-mentioned trucking downcycle, which has lasted for at least 3 years (since early 2023), the company's operating margins have come under significant pressure, breaking the breakeven line this year.

This thin margin is not enough to cover the company's interest expenses on a GAAP basis. The company is not super levered, with $600 million in debts and leases for a company with $1.3 billion in tangible assets, but the margin pressure is high at this time of the cycle.

The company can cover interest and maintain a relatively breakeven FCF because it has been cutting CAPEX significantly (as compared to D&A below). Part of D&A includes amortization of intangibles from acquisitions for about $46 million per year (see the 3Q25 earnings release for 9M25 figures, annualized). These kinds of intangibles do not really deplete, so the business should not be damaged by lower investment. Still, without margin improvements, the company will find itself in more cash flow challenges.

Low Returns on Capital

Although RXO's debts are inherited from the time of the spin-off, I do not know if the business should have any debt at all. Its returns on capital are low, especially during cyclical downturns, and therefore, interest can become a burden.

Calculating the capital returns for RXO is not trivial. The company calls itself 'asset-light' because it does not need trucks, but when we consider its operating income over assets (my preferred measure of return), we find that its capital return is relatively low (6% pre-tax below or ~4.5% post-tax).

However, some might argue that total assets is not representative of the company's capital needs because it includes intangibles and goodwill (about 50% of the balance sheet historically), coming from the spin-off and from acquisitions (like last year's acquisition of UPS' UK brokerage business, Coyote). If we removed these, then the return on (tangible) assets would be at least 2x, or about 12% on average—not that bad.

I can agree with that view on a go-forward, no-further-acquisition basis. If the company froze its acquisitions today, it is possible that its businesses would return closer to 12% tangible assets. However, if the company uses capital (equity, debt, or earnings) to acquire businesses and pays a premium on those businesses, then it will keep a low return on invested capital.

Without the acquisition of Coyote in 2024 (a press release indicates the business generated about $3.2 billion in revenues in 2023), the company (only $2 billion above its 2023 revenues) would not have grown at all and even potentially shrunk.

Valuation Under Both Capital Scenarios

In order to value the company, we can work with both capital scenarios, because they yield the same result today and historically, albeit not the same going forward.

Because using total assets (including intangibles and goodwill) or using tangible assets modifies the denominator, the higher return on assets is offset by a lower asset base. Using a 6% average return on a total asset base of $3 billion is the same as using a 12% average return on a tangible asset base of $1.5 billion; both indicate an average operating income of $180 million. After interest of about $35 million and taxes of 25%, we get to $110 million in net income. Compared to a market cap of $2.42 billion, this already does not look great (an earnings yield of only 4.5%). Even if we add back the non-cash amortization expense for $46 million, we get $156 million in cash-distributable income, or a yield of 6.5%, below the market.

Only if we adopted extreme optimism and based our calculations on 2022 cycle-peak margins (11% over the total asset base) would we get a moderate 11% yield ($3 billion in assets results in $330 million in operating income, which, after taxes and interest, becomes ~$220 million, to which we add $45 million in non-cash intangibles amortization).

That is, without growth, under regular assumptions (still optimistic because they assume a return to the median in the cycle), we get a 6.5% distributable yield, and only after high optimism assumptions, a 10%+ yield. This is for a company that today can barely cover interest and that I believe has very little in terms of competitive advantages.

In order to add growth, the picture gets more complicated. The company has not grown organically since the spin-off, and inorganic growth requires paying premiums that become intangibles (the Coyote acquisition was almost purely intangibles, according to the 10-K).

Assuming constant returns (over total assets in this case), in order to grow 5%, the company would expand its balance sheet (mainly intangibles via acquisitions, plus some receivables) at 5% per year. However, because the post-tax returns on assets are less than 5% (4.5% as expressed above), the company cannot self-finance that 5% of growth. It has to either issue equity, as it did last year, diluting its shareholders, or issue debt, which is risky.

If the company used all of the $156 million in distributable cash as calculated above, that would basically yield about 5% growth in the balance sheet, but at the expense of receiving no other source of return.

That is, at current prices, even assuming a return to the mean, RXO only promises an MSD kind of return, either from earnings without growth, or from growth (without distributable earnings). I think this is an extremely low return for a stock that is risky if the cycle doesn't turn and that earns a subpar return on total capital.

For these reasons, I initiate RXO with a Hold rating.

data.title: Sanlorenzo: Luxury Positioning With Strong Visibility
data.description:
For the first time, we moved on to analysing a yacht manufacturer. While this may appear unconventional for our readers, it is a natural extension of our long-standing focus on luxury goods. Sanlorenzo (SNLRF) is a well-established name recognized for the quality of its yachts and the strength of its leadership, particularly its CEO.

Back to basics, given it's our initiation of coverage, we start with the company profile. Sanlorenzo is an Italian shipbuilder focused on the design, production, and commercialisation of bespoke luxury yachts. Taking advantage of the H1 results presentation, the company is divided into four business units across three distinct brands (Fig. 1): 1 Sanlorenzo brand covers yachts and 2) superyachts, 3) Bluegame (sport utility yachts), and 4) Nautor Swan (sailing yachts).

Source: Sanlorenzo H1 results ppt - Fig 1

Why are we Positive?

Sanlorenzo published its results in mid-November 2025, and much of the disclosed information is already reflected in market expectations. As customary, the analysis looks ahead, with the following sections outlining the key forward-looking highlights:

The group is a long-established Italian yacht builder, drawing on 67 years of craftsmanship in the luxury segment. In aggregate, Sanlorenzo generates €930 million in top-line from new yacht deliveries within a global boating market of roughly €35 billion (source: Italian Marine Industry Association and Deloitte). According to the report, Italy remains the world leader in Superyacht production with a 54% market share. The company’s “made-to-measure” model is highly customized and combines standardized exterior platforms. This has scale benefits and supports cost optimisation while reducing execution risk. Here at the Lab, we particularly appreciate its positioning in the 30–50 meter range. Indeed, yachts are well-suited to Mediterranean cruising while still capable of transatlantic use, including the Caribbean. Aside from physical limits on the yacht's length, a few regions are starting to restrict where vessels can operate. So, we believe there is less strategic appeal in the >100 meters yacht market.

Q3 2025 results were mixed. Top-line sales came in short of Wall Street expectations, and management attributed it to higher demand in the larger 30m segment (Fig. 2). This will require longer build times and will delay P&L sales. Based on our intelligence, this is not a negative structural signal, and we believe Sanlorenzo's mid-term growth profile remains attractive.

Source: Sanlorenzo Q3 results ppt - Fig 2

The company's Q3 order book showed renewed momentum. The company recorded year-to-date order book growth of 18.4% (Fig. 3). The backlog provides meaningful visibility and is a clear positive sign in our investment case. We should report that Sanlorenzo’s products and innovations have been a key contributor to stronger order intake. This will likely support both its earnings growth story.

Fig 3

Key to the report is that scarcity and direct distribution remain central to Sanlorenzo's equity story. The Sanlorenzo standalone brand accounted for about 80% of the company's totalsales in 2024. The company sold 69 new yachts. Roughly half of the group's top-line sales come from repeat customers, who tend to upgrade every 4–5 years. This provides a key upside to us. Additionally, 76% of these clients decided to increase their expenses compared to their prior purchase. This implies earnings visibility and lower intermediary costs.

We also see Sanlorenzo distribution as a key differentiator. We like the company's capital strategic priorities. The latest acquisition of Simpson Marine (adding 14 direct points of presence in APAC) and the build-out of the U.S. footprint, supported by the American Magic partnership, should accelerate growth in both regions. The Americas are expected to account for around 21% of sales in 2025, up from 16% in 2024.

Lastly, Sanlorenzo's valuation appears undemanding at this stage. The company's shares trade at approximately a 10x P/E on 2026 estiamtes. While a double-digit P/E is not cheap, we believe Sanlorenzo is more of a luxury peerthan an industrial player. Valuation is 5x lower than its long-term average, despite a 6% EPS CAGR. Therefore, we see this as an attractive entry point. Additionally, the company paid a €1 dividend, which may grow this year (yield 3.3%), and there is an ongoing buyback, further underpinning our investment case.

Earnings and Valuation

We expect Sanlorenzo to continue expanding in sales in 2026 (Fig. 4). This is supported by the company's backlog and its M&A activities. Going down to the P&L, we see EBIT growth both in absolute terms and on a percentage basis. With the new product MIX (New Swan Alloy), we believe Sanlorenzo might target€1 billion in sales (a 4% top-line sales growth vs. 2025 numbers). Even applying the same EBIT margin (14.6%), Sanlorenzo can achieve a €146 million in core operating profit. With a 30% tax rate, our 2026 net profit estimate is €102.2 million. This implied an EPS of €2.87 and a P/E of 10.5x. This does not include: more deliveries, a higher EBIT margin, potential positive interest in 2026 given FCF evolution and no debt (Fig. 5), and no lower outstanding shares. In the Q3 call, management noted that net debt is closely linked to NWC trends and that by year-end, they expect a favorable net financial position.

Going to the valuation, Sanlorenzo's historical P/E has been above 15x. At this stage, the company trades at a 2026 P/E of 10.5x and an EV/EBITDA of 5.5x. This is not representative of our luxury coverage. Assigning a valuation multiple is invariably challenging, as it requires balancing forward expectations with inherently uncertain assumptions. So, we use a blend valuation methodology with a target multiple of 7.5x on the EBITDA and a P/E of 12x. We derive a target price of €39 per share, representing a 25% upside from the current level.

Fig 4

Fig 5

Risks

Sanlorenzo downside risks include macroeconomic and geopolitical volatility; however, we see limited disruption in sales. The company might be exposed to potential shifts in consumer preferences; however, these products are tailor-made. Sanlorenzo is exposed to supply-chain interruptions due to its significant reliance on external suppliers, capacity bottlenecks, and additional ERG requirements. Sanlorenzo risks include higher competitive pressure in a relatively concentrated market and execution risk on the latest M&A deal.

Conclusion

We like the sector and the Sanlorenzo subsegment within the industry. The company valuation is not demanding, and we have not included potential upside in our investment case. Therefore, we initiate coverage of Sanlorenzo with a clear buy rating.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 01/15/26
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: EOG Resources: Building Out Natural Gas Production Will Help It Power Data Centers
data.description:
I last wrote about EOG Resources (EOG) in February of 2025. In that article I basically pointed out that EOG Resources was a safe, yet boring investment. They have a good dividend yield, a very solid balance sheet, and have just been profitably expanding for decades. They have not been a fast grower and, quite frankly, do little to excite investors, as you aren't likely to get 100%+ returns in the next year investing in EOG, like you might if you pick the right AI-related names.

I still think EOG is the perfect place to park unused capital, especially if you find most of your capital concentrated on AI names or the Mag7 (so that would also include if the majority of your money is invested in the S&P or Nasdaq). EOG has a near 4% dividend that has been growing at a CAGR around 20% for the last 5 years, so they comfortably beat even elevated inflation we've been seeing. And although, as I mentioned above, you may get 100%+ returns if you pick the right AI names, you can also lose 80-100% of your investment if you pick the wrong ones. That just isn't the case for EOG. Because EOG is in a commodity industry, they do still see big drawdowns, but those are always temporary, and they always bounce back. You can keep collecting the dividends while you wait.

EOG has been underperforming the market by a wide margin, with terrible 2025 returns, but it's not the short-term share price movements that you care about if you buy into EOG. To me, it is the place to park capital if you find many other parts of the market ahead of themselves but don't want to have too much capital in cash due to inflation.

I personally still think the Mag 7 will do very well over the next 5 years or 10 years. Maybe not all of them, but most of them, due to the ways they'll be able to save money from AI and get extra synergies that smaller companies can't manage. I also think there will be many AI names that will win over the next decade, and returns on them will be fantastic; picking the right ones will be a lot harder. However, I am definitely not putting all my capital in the tech or the Mag 7, in case there is an AI bubble and it gets popped in the coming years. Also, I think the Mag 7 could have a tough next 1-3 years, even if they do well over the next 5-10, which is another reason I'm moving more of my money into other names in the short term.

Diversification and the Next Chapter in the AI Story

It's always important to stay diversified if you want to protect your capital. Being 100% invested in AI names would have worked exceedingly well over the last few years, but the party always comes to a stop eventually. I'm sure people justified staying invested in tech names before the dot-com bubble, but we all know how much those names suffered for many years after that. I'm not saying AI is 100% akin to the dot-com bubble, but there are some similarities.

Even if the big tech companies continue to keep pouring CAPEX money into expanding their AI capabilities for the foreseeable future, I expect the companies that win from that will start changing. NVIDIA (NVDA) was, of course, the big winner for a couple of years, along with many others such as Alphabet (GOOG), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and Nebius (NBIS) really taking off in 2025 as other big winners have started to become more clear.

Basically, for the most part, the winners have been chip/graphics card makers, hyperscalers, and those building out servers/data centers. There have been some energy-related names that have started to be winners from AI, such as Bloom Energy (BE), as they have made deals to help supply clean energy for data centers. At some point I expect this will spread to all energy names that provide any sort of energy for data centers. Oil may not be as important for this, but natural gas is still very important to powering the infrastructure that is being built out to serve AI needs.

And that's where I get back to EOG. They are still primarily an oil company, but they also produce a large amount of natural gas, and they have been looking to expand their natural gas production. In just the last year, they have increased their natural gas production from 1,970 MMcfd in Q3 2024 to 2,745 MMcfd in Q3 2025, good for almost a 40% increase Y/Y. With their natural gas growing at a much faster pace these last few years than their oil production, this has put their mix right around 70% oil and 30% natural gas in the most recent quarter. This strategy seems to be in line with the fact that the United States is undergoing some amount of electrification, lessening the need for oil, but building out data centers at a very rapid rate, increasing the need for natural gas and other cleaner forms of energy.

Great Entry Point

From a technical perspective, I like EOG a lot too. I look at technical analysis as secondary to fundamental analysis, but if I like a company's fundamentals, I think taking a technical look can offer some extra info into when the best entry points may be. Here is a look at EOG over the last 5 years:

EOG has had a pretty hard floor of $100 over the last 4 years. Technically, if they break under this, they could go lower, but when I'm just buying for the dividend and holding for the long term, I'm just looking for good entry points. I consider this one of them. Either they hit their floor of the last few years, they go higher, and I lock in at one of the higher dividend yields I could grab, or they go lower and I wait for the next good entry point to buy more. I am still leaving some money on the side and not making this quite a "full" position. This is also how they have looked over the last few decades:

Outside of the pandemic that sent oil prices briefly negative because of how much supply chains and general world transportation were disrupted, EOG has just kept marching higher for decades. I think this is from their approach of continual, slow, profitable growth. Some oil and gas companies take on tons of debt to make acquisitions and try to grow as much as they can during boom times.

I see EOG as a company that always makes the fiscally responsible decisions and never takes on much risk. This is kind of ironic, since EOG (which stands for Enron Oil and Gas) Resources was spun off (in 1999) from the Enron that was a part of so many scandals in 2001. EOG Resources was never a part of the scandals. I just thought it was a stark contrast between EOG Resources, which has always been fiscally responsible, and their former parent company, Enron, which was involved in the big accounting scandals, but I digress.

To show just how fiscally responsible EOG has been over the years, here are their debt-to-equity and debt-to-EBITDA ratios compared to some of their peers:

EOG has still taken on debt when they've needed to or they've deemed it a good growth opportunity that offers high enough returns. But they have been very conservative, especially over the last 10 years, which really showed its importance during Covid, as the other oil producers debt levels shot much higher.

Where is Oil Going?

I have seen arguments for oil prices going up and for them going down. On the one hand, drilling of shale formations, fracking, and other technological advances have opened up many more ways to keep extracting oil without expensive new megaprojects and exploration needed. This has become apparent as the US has become the world's leading oil producer in recent years, as its output has surged for more than a decade. Add the issue of electrification driving down the need for as much oil in the future, as well as the fact that OPEC has already been holding back considerable capacity, and oil bear arguments have some real merit.

On the other side, they would argue that there has been underinvestment in oil for many years and that eventually all the cheap, easy-to-access spots unlocked by new technologies will have been used up. For now and likely for many decades, the world is still very dependent on oil. Some of the most developed nations are working to be less reliant on it, but for now, oil is still a very inelastic resource. The price has to go up a lot before people stop buying as much of it, since it is generally used to commute to jobs and provide the main way to live lives the way people are used to.

To get to the point of this section, I don't know at all where oil prices are going. I tend to think they could still go down further in the short term. There does seem to be a supply glut that OPEC can't just make up for by taking some oil offline, as they already have quite a bit offline. The United States, Canada, and some other non-OPEC nations have expanded their production so much in the last decade or two that it could still take a while to even out. However, the longer the price of oil stays low or goes lower, the more I think it will jump up in the future. And EOG still has enough natural gas production to keep the next few years looking bright.

Conclusion

It doesn't matter too much where oil goes in the short term, because EOG offers an investment that has very good dividends through whatever happens in the short term and a real option-like return possible if oil ever jumps up in the medium or long term. The longer oil stays at a reasonable range and oil E&P companies have subpar returns, the more I think they will pay off if and when demand ever outstrips supply for a longer period of time.

EOG is a company with a stellar balance sheet, a very long history through all sorts of oil environments, and great margins that should help it stay profitable even if oil prices fall from their current levels. They are one of my top picks in the sector to park money for steadying dividends and a possible big upside if held for the years and decades ahead. I think oil will eventually surge at some point over the next decade, but until that time, EOG is focusing on increasing their natural gas production, which should do well for the next few years.

data.title: TBG: Why I'm Not Sold On This High-Conviction Dividend ETF
data.description:
Investment Thesis

This article continues my coverage of the TBG Dividend Focus ETF (TBG), last reviewed six months ago. In this update, I will reiterate that TBG offers few meaningful advantages over a similar-yielding passive fund like the Fidelity High Dividend ETF (FDVV). Although I am interested in seeing how TBG's managers adapt over a full market cycle, I recommend interested investors take a "watch and see" approach for now. Therefore, I've rated it a "hold" today, with plans to downgrade it in 2026 should its fundamentals and relative performance against FDVV not improve.

TBG Overview

TBG is an actively managed fund comprised of 30-40 stocks with a record of consistently increasing their dividends. The fund manager's active approach uses quantitative and qualitative measures to assess dividend sustainability and growth potential. Underpinning TBG's strategy is the belief that dividend growth and quality are strongly connected, and if a company can increase its dividends by more than 5% per year over a 5-7 year market cycle, there's a good chance it's on solid financial footing.

TBG's expense ratio is high at 0.59%, as is its 0.09% 30-day median bid/ask spread. As of January 13, 2026, TBG's 30-day SEC yield is 3.26%, and the fund makes distributions quarterly, with its most recent dividend including a $0.0679 per share capital gain. REITs are eligible holdings, so not all of TBG's distributions are qualified.

TBG Dividends and Performance

TBG shareholders saw a 25% increase in dividends last year. However, most was manufactured growth. New ETFs often have volatile dividend growth rates, and large percentage changes in shares outstanding can distort per-share payments. This is evident because TBG's current portfolio is three-quarters similar to last year, and the common holdings only increased their dividends by 7% in aggregate last year. That's in line with the manager's stated 5-7% objective, but I don't want readers to get the impression that 25% annual dividend growth is sustainable. It almost certainly is not, absent deliberate and regular rebalances to higher-yielding securities.

TBG's performance track record is short, but between December 2023 and December 2025, it managed to outperform the Schwab U.S. Dividend Equity ETF (SCHD) by an annualized 5.32% (16.13% vs. 10.81%). TBG also lagged behind FDVV by 5.41% with more volatility, leading to inferior risk-adjusted and downside risk-adjusted returns (Sharpe and Sortino Ratios).

It's no coincidence that FDVV was the better performer over this bullish period, since it's more growth-oriented than TBG and SCHD. FDVV uses what I call a "barbell" approach to dividend investing, which involves selecting high- and low-yielding stocks that are loosely correlated. This approach allows for a respectable 2.77% estimated dividend yield even though Nvidia (NVDA) and Apple (AAPL) are its top two holdings. Meanwhile, TBG and SCHD hold stocks with roughly the same dividend yields, but the key difference is TBG's 0.59% expense ratio. This high fee reduces TBG's expected dividend yield to just 3.38%, or only 0.61% more than FDVV's. In my view, this is not enough extra income to matter.

TBG Sector Allocations vs. FDVV and SCHD

As of January 10, 2026, TBG had 18% and 14% allocated to the Health Care and Energy sectors. Compared to SCHD and FDVV, TBG is underweight Technology by 8% on average, and overall, it's very well diversified at the sector level. Still, I'm somewhat surprised, given how TBG markets itself as an active fund applying a "bottom-up and sector-agnostic" strategy. With turnover rates of only 8% and 26% the last two years, it seems that TBG has settled into a well-diversified portfolio it intends to mostly keep moving forward. Therefore, intentional or not, I believe diversification is key to the strategy.

TBG Fundamentals vs. FDVV: More Weaknesses Than Strengths

In my thesis, I mentioned how TBG holds few meaningful advantages over FDVV from a fundamentals perspective. Broadly, here is how I have the two funds ranked on five key fundamental factors:

This table indicates that TBG is less risky and has more value factor exposure than FDVV. In contrast, TBG's 2.16/10 growth score is substantially lower than FDVV's 4.84/10 score, and it's also slightly deficient on quality and momentum at the moment. Given how we know growth and momentum stocks have led markets over the last few years, these scores align with the performance differences we saw earlier.

The underlying statistics for each fund support this conclusion. On a weighted average basis, TBG's components have sales and earnings growth rates that are roughly half those of FDVV's. Given the fund manager's 5-7% dividend growth goal over a full cycle, TBG's 4.34% three-year EPS CAGR is slightly low, and this will need to improve to avoid stretching the payout ratio to uncomfortable levels. Already, TBG's 65.50% ratio is high, and FDVV appears to have more leeway to increase dividends due to its lower payout ratio and higher earnings growth rates.

TBG and FDVV have comparable weighted free cash flow margins of around 14%, but from an operations and capital efficiency perspective, FDVV's EBIT margins and ROTC ratio are materially better.

TBG's growth and quality gaps relative to FDVV have more to do with what it underweights rather than what it overweights. As shown below, TBG has zero exposure to ten stocks that, in aggregate, increased their earnings per share by an annualized 17% over the last three years, have a payout ratio of just 25%, and have free cash flow margins of 25%. These stocks comprise 34% of FDVV, and while it's true that they only yield 1.06% on average, it's a massive fundamental tradeoff for only a marginal 0.61% net improvement in yield.

Risks to Thesis

TBG and FDVV are subject to change, so these fundamental differences might not hold moving forward. TBG also currently has advantages over FDVV on value and risk, which could prove beneficial if market enthusiasm declines in 2026. To illustrate how, consider statistics like TBG's 0.76 five-year portfolio beta and 18.91x trailing P/E ratio, which suggest it's less risky and more value-oriented than FDVV. Rather than having Nvidia and Apple on its top ten holdings list, TBG holds several defensive Health Care names like Merck & Co. (MRK) and Johnson & Johnson (JNJ).

To assess the likelihood of a market slowdown, I track S&P 500 quarterly earnings surprises. Historically, decelerating or below-average surprises often precede valuation re-ratings or market crashes, with the latest example being in mid/late 2021. As shown below, earnings surprises began to decelerate from their post-pandemic highs, and ultimately, the index's TTM P/E declined by 15% in 2022, per this S&P report.

The Technology sector's P/E compressed further by 26% that year, so given FDVV's +17% overweight in that sector, I can see how negative sentiment could lead to TBG outperforming. However, with surprises consistently above average, it's too early to make that call, so it's not my base case.

Investment Recommendation

I reiterate my view that FDVV is superior to TBG for dividend investors. Its 2.77% expected yield is only 0.61% lower than TBG's, and this difference is more than offset by material advantages in growth and quality. TBG's 0.59% expense ratio is another important headwind that negatively impacts total returns and distributions, so while today's "hold" rating reflects my patience for seeing how the active fund can adapt to changing market conditions, I anticipate downgrading TBG to a "sell" sometime in 2026 if its fundamentals and performance do not improve.

data.title: Vermilion Energy: A Deep-Value Natural Gas Opportunity Poised To Benefit From Macro Tailwinds
data.description:
Introduction

Last time I covered Vermilion Energy (VET), I highlighted their significant discount to fair and book value and ongoing portfolio rebalancing, transitioning into long-life, high-quality natural gas assets across Canada and Europe and targeting a balanced approach including debt reductions and solid dividends.

Despite a ~15% increase since that article was published a couple of months ago, I still believe VET is a Strong Buy, offering a compelling opportunity to gain exposure to long-term tailwinds at a very good price, potentially supported by recent macro developments as well.

Internal Developments

As a note, remember that they report data in CAD. As we can see, the company reported solid cash flows from operating activities, while continuing to spend a lot on drilling and development but also receiving C$483.53 million from dispositions.

The company recorded C$108.25 million in free cash flow during Q3 (vs C$126.1 million in Q3'24), bringing the 9M level to C$274.78 million (vs C$354.99 million over 9M'24), meaning roughly US$197.84 in the first 9 months of 2025 when converted into USD. As a note, I'm using FFO minus E&D CAPEX to get to this FCF, as they also define it.

The dispositions are the results of Vermilion's streamlining their operations through the sale of their non-core operations in the US (completing their US exit), as well as light oil operations in Saskatchewan and Manitoba, freeing very significant amounts of cash that can be used for their debt repayment initiatives (as they say), cleaning their balance sheet while developing their Montney and Deep Basin assets.

Regarding that, based on VET's latest report, we can see a solid financial position even though the current assets aren't covering the current liabilities now, with significant debt repayments compared to the C$1.95 billion in long-term debt seen in Q2. The C$2.71 billion equity (US$1.95 billion) is also significantly above the US$1.28 billion market cap, potentially hinting at a good margin of safety, although we should keep in mind that the value of these assets ultimately depends on their profitability in the everchanging oil and gas environment.

Regarding the long-term debt, we can see it being spread very well into the future, with their notes being due all the way in 2030 and 2033, so they have a lot of time to finish developing their projects and enjoy the cash flows they would generate before having to repay or refinance their outstanding debt.

The company's dividend remains at a solid ~4.48% yield, with the company's CFO mentioning their intention to increase it by 4% in 2026 and highlighting the following during their Q3 Earnings Call, which I agree with and consider to be a very good approach:

Our priorities on shareholder returns remain unchanged. We will use excess free cash flow to maintain a strong balance sheet, fund a sustainable base dividend and be opportunistic with share buybacks. I'm pleased to announce our intention to increase the quarterly cash dividend by 4% to CAD 0.135 per share, effective with the Q1 2026 dividend. The dividend payout remains at a modest level even during this commodity price period, and we see the potential for higher return of capital, as free cash flow increases in the Montney, Germany and Deep Basin.

Macro Developments

Although it may not seem like such a big deal for natural gas, the Venezuela situation can still have ramifications on the broader industry since it can affect oil prices, resulting in lower capital for oil and gas companies to develop projects/wells, potentially leading to less natural gas supply in an environment where demand could grow thanks to tailwinds including global LNG trade, AI energy demand, etc., which can actually be a positive development.

However, this remains an uncertain situation and the timing remains impossible to predict, as even if the US manages to replace the country's leadership quickly, US oil companies will likely not rush in to enter the market, as there's still significant political and economic uncertainty.

Despite political statements such as White House spokeswoman Taylor Rogers' "all of our oil companies are ready and willing to make big investments in Venezuela that will rebuild their oil infrastructure," the reality will likely be very different, as these companies know very well that their actions can impact the price.

Especially in an already quite weak oil market, oil from Venezuela flooding the market can be a significant threat, even though it's bad in terms of quality and requires a lot of refining, and companies like Exxon (XOM) calling Venezuela currently "uninvestable" summarizes their stance quite well.

Despite the situation in Venezuela, OPEC reaffirmed their plan to hold production steady through Q1 2026, which can help oil prices stabilize, as further production hikes could've continued the commodity's crash. Meanwhile, the significant socio-political tension in Iran can also be a factor to keep in mind, with oil prices rising by 2.5% on the day of writing this due to supply concerns following a similar fall the day before.

However, I also believe it's important to note that even before this whole situation, JPMorgan warned that oil could go into the $30s by late 2027 (a more extreme scenario backed by significant oversupply), with more stable scenarios hinting at WTI prices averaging around $53/bbl to $54/bbl in 2026 and 2027.

Another opportunity that can stem from this is the potential to see the market's interest move away from the US due to political and economic risks intensifying.

As I mentioned before, several studies showed the continued pressure on the US consumer (rising costs of living, lower income, etc.), while uncertainty around the Fed's independence mounted up following the DOJ criminal investigation into Powell, with Bank of America's CEO also warning that the market will "punish people" if the Fed's independence is affected once Powell's term ends in May 2026.

What all of this can result is eroding the market's trust in the country's safe haven status, potentially leading to higher costs of debt/capital combined with money moving into other alternatives to diversify - including Canada.

Especially with Canadian gas prices facing extremely cheap levels, it wouldn't be impossible to see data center and infrastructure demand grow in the region, although that will depend significantly on the country's infrastructure. As a note here, VET managed to deliver a peer-leading realized gas price, as AECO prices only represented 43% of their price exposure in Q3, with significant hedges and European exposure helping them outperform.

About that, there's been some potentially good news recently thanks to Canada's "Grand Bargain" that has potential to revitalize the country's oil and gas industry. To summarize, this is a Memorandum of Understanding between the Canadian federal government and the Alberta government, essentially being a trade-off where the latter gets federal backing for a new oil pipeline to the Pacific (unlocking the huge potential for Asian market exports) and strengthen the power grids to supply low-carbon electricity to LNG facilities while it agrees to carbon pricing and carbon capture/storage/etc. to reduce the emissions this would generate.

Although the main headline oil pipeline is primarily for heavy oil/bitumen and VET produces light oil and condensate, if the pipeline improves the overall basin takeaway capacity and economics, we could see condensate (used to dilute bitumen) improving alongside it, which VET does produce as a byproduct of its liquids-rich gas. Not to mention the potential to boost the broader industry as a result and attract more investments.

But again, keep in mind that it's just an MoU for now and things can certainly change, but it has potential to be a significant step forward.

Risks Reiterated

Despite the company's long-term potential, we still have to be aware of the current environment, with AECO prices facing their lowest levels ever recently (falling below zero in September), as the market is struggling with a supply glut that's basically waiting for LNG export capacity like LNG Canada to fully ramp up - among others (being expected to recover in 2026).

This macro pressure can lead to VET slowing down their production and projects significantly, potentially hurting their cash flow severely - which is why their focus on debt repayments is good now, basically improving their resilience as they might be forced into a self-preservation mode.

Reiterated Valuation

Once again, I'll convert everything into USD from the start, while the overall valuation hasn't changed that much since the company continued to advance as expected. To be significantly more conservative from the start and assume pressure from the current environment, I'll assume -$100 million in FCF in 2025 to have a better margin of safety given the CAPEX expectations and pressure from the environment, even though the 9M FCF is much higher already, just to show the significant disconnection in current prices.

Then, given their expectations of 40% higher production in 2026 vs. 2024, I'll assume that they can make only $200 million (below 2024 levels) in FCF during 2026, which should be higher if their commodity price expectations are true, with similar CAPEX (inflated for project expansions), remaining flat until 2028. This would then grow to $300 million thanks to Montney (below their expectations) and other projects materializing, followed by a 3% CAGR until year 10, plus a 2% terminal growth rate, even if natural gas likely has much better potential. I'll go for a discount rate of 12% and 154.92 million shares outstanding as of the Q3 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $2.44 billion, for an intrinsic value of $15.76 per VET share, or about C$21.88 for VET:CA, which is significantly above the current levels even with the conservative assumptions we used.

Conclusion

Vermilion remains a Strong Buy, offering a healthy, improving and clearly undervalued way to gain exposure to solid assets that can benefit from long-term tailwinds and energy demand. There are of course risks since we're talking about a commodity company facing very significant macro pressure right now, but the potential to improve from here is just as significant, in my opinion.

data.title: Fortitude Gold Flashing Turnaround Signals
data.description:
Fortitude gold (FTCO) exhibits promising turnaround potential after enjoying a strong run at the Isabella Pearl mine only to be stalled by permit delays that curtailed significant production last year. Investors should focus on the significant potential margins on gold production in this historic price rise, while noting future cash flow per share, with only 24.5 million shares outstanding. The business model was set up to capitalize on the potential to distribute significant dividends back to stockholders.

Management mapped a plan to continuously layer yearly mining of additional low cost high-margin open pit satellite deposits only to be derailed by unrelenting permit delays during 2020 through 2024. As a result, gold production has decreased significantly over the past two years, with the Isabella Pearl mine approaching depletion. FTCO balance sheet remains strong with $11.7 million in cash with very limited share dilution. This is unique among gold miners.

A catalyst has arrived with three recent permit approvals that will unlock gold at Scarlet South and County Line deposits that will supplement the high grade gold currently being mined in the deep Pearl zone.

In 2024, FTCO filed for a permit modification to mine 60 meters deeper within Isabella Pearl. A lengthy bureaucratic delay ensued with final approval in April of 2025. This deep mining initiative was a fallback option to generate production until the permits arrived for the two new gold deposits. Now the company will alternate their labor force between Pearl and Scarlet which lies just 600 meters to the north. Production should rebound steadily with ore coming from three separate deposits.

In 2018, County Line gold property in Nevada was acquired for $300,000 and a 3% net smelter royalty. Fortitude Gold (FTCO) transported the first batch of ore for processing last week as gold prices surged close to $4,600. The company stands to generate close to $186 million in free cash flow over the next 4 years which will be allocated among only 24.375 million shares outstanding. This would equate to $7.63 in FCF per share. I held gold price flat at $4,500 per ounce in a hypothetical. The stock currently trades at $5.27 per share with a market cap at $134 million.

There are 50,000 ounces of gold listed as mineral resources at the property (as of 2022), with at least two open pit targets. The mining economics are highly profitable at an open pit heap leach mine with high relative grades at ~1 gram.

Fortitude employs a disciplined effective approach of acquiring low cost heap leach gold along the Walker Lane mineral trend in southwest Nevada. The prolific Isabella Pearl deposit delivered sizeable dividends that reached $.48 per share annually in 2023.

FTCO made a bold decision to terminate their existing mining contract in an effort to save money, such as the contractor “mark up”. Embedded within that contract was the “lease” charge for equipment. Management can now more efficiently move their labor between multiple sites without experiencing costly added charges.

The company held $11.7 million in cash at end of September 2025 with modest “lease financing debt” of $17.2 million, scheduled to be paid in yearly installments at $6.5 million in both 2026 and 2027 and $4.2 million in 2028. The lease financing debt represents company ownership of equipment.

On January 7th the company began harvesting ore from County Line (13 miles away), which sent its first truckload back to Pearl for processing. With high grade low cost ore available at multiple deposits, management can balance mine plan sequence to maintain an efficient grade level on the heap leach pad, while beginning to harvest significant cash levels in the current gold rush era.

County Line Deposit

To provide a conservative analysis of forward cash flow potential, I used the company's mineral resource (2022) at 50,000 ounces and conservatively added 10,000 from the recently drilled “East pit” -which has not yet been included in the stated resource. The company anticipates updating this new resource in the coming weeks and has stated that mining from this pit will occur in tandem with operations at the main historic deposit.

I allocated production of 15,000 ounces annually over 4-year period to balance production and demonstrate the powerful value underlying the current stock.

15,000 ounces at $4,500 gold price equals $67,575,000 less all-in sustaining costs estimated at $1,400 per ounce ($21,000,000), leading to a gross annual profit of $46,575,00 or $1.91 per share. If the gold price drops by 30% to $3,380, the company remains cash flow rich, with a margin of approximately $2,000 per ounce.

My production scenario above is intentionally set well below the potential listed within the company presentation slide below, which indicates annual gold production at approximately 22,500 ounces thru 2028. It appears that one would have to add some “lost” production in 2025 (permit delay) into the forward years that would move the annual number closer to 30,000 ounces annually.

I understate the company's cash flow potential, as I have included zero production from these additional mining operations below, both of which hold gold. The company has not released a mineral resource for these two deposits.

1)Isabella Pearl deep mining is underway. The Pearl deep zone host some high grade gold but the company has not released a mineral resource for this short-term operation expected to conclude by midyear. The high grade zone of mineralization is expected to be reached shortly, as mining works through the final benches.

2)Scarlet South deposit mining that commences in this first quarter. Scarlet south looks like a promising low-cost heap leach operation, with gold appearing near the surface within the drill results. Heap leach gold with moderate strip (waste) ratios, offer dynamic profit margins.

Fortitude CEO Jason Reid earlier stated, “We are prioritizing Scarlet with the upcoming drill campaign looking to delineate enough mineralization to advance the project towards a production decision. Scarlet’s very close proximity to our operating mine, with a short haul distance to both the heap leach and waste rock facility at Isabella Pearl, could allow for a project production decision based primarily on the economics of removing the overburden to access this mineralization. Scarlet’s proximity effectively lowers the hurdles and minimizes the project capital needs to reach a production decision due to leveraging nearby process infrastructure.”

The company is leveraging the existing process facilities at Isabella Pearl to reduce capital expenditure costs involved with building new plants at these satellite locations, including both Scarlet South and County Line. Finally, the Golden Mile mine (150,000 ounces -mineral reserves) permit is under review and on a fast track designation with approval potential as early as the Spring of 2027.

Dividends

Fortitude intends to pay substantial dividends to stockholders as soon as significant free cash flow returns. This approach serves to draw investors who seek high yields and may not normally target mining stocks. FTCO dividend previously reached $.48 per share in 2023 with a gold price averaging only $1,900 per ounce. At current price of $4,626 the opportunity to ramp up the current annual dividend of $.12 is significant.

Gold price rise

In our current financial situation, where national debt is $37 trillion and growing every day, there is no better time to hold at least a percentage of your portfolio in companies that produce real money known as gold. The dollar is a derivative of gold and has lost 50% of its purchasing power over the past 5 years, while the gold price has more than doubled. There is no evident appetite to restrain spending or balance a budget, let alone trim spending. The gold price will likely move higher in reaction to continued devaluation of the dollar. Fortitude's stock price stands to gain as production ramps up in 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Rigetti Computing: A Falling Knife, Not A 'Buy-The-Dip' Opportunity
data.description:
Reasons to continue avoiding RGTI

The fact that my previous Strong Sell call on Rigetti Computing (RGTI) was published on October 15, the day when the stock hit its all-time high, is just a funny coincidence. However, the fact that RGTI's share price has been melting rapidly since then looks justified from the fundamental perspective. There are various solid indications that RGTI is still a dream away from commercialization, meaning that its $8 billion valuation is a purely speculative figure. I am confident in this assertion because without commercialization it is simply impossible to justify a multi-billion market cap.

For example, Wall Street analysts expect RGTI's revenue to increase from $7.6 million in FY2025 to $93.5 million in FY2028, more than a ten-fold growth. Such aggressive projected revenue growth itself is already an ultra-optimistic assumption. However, even if RGTI achieves this growth, it means that the forward P/S ratio is 87. Some people might say that it is not very high compared to Palantir's current P/S ratio of approximately 100. However, I would like to recommend avoiding such comparisons because RGTI and Palantir are in different leagues. Palantir already surpassed $1 billion in quarterly sales, it demonstrates stellar profitability, and its validation flywheel is strong. Therefore, such comparisons are absolutely irrelevant, as RGTI's sales are just slightly above zero, its EPS is not expected to become positive in the foreseeable future, and commercialization timing is highly uncertain.

Since there is no positive forward EPS available for the next few years, the only other valuation metric that is available to assess RGTI's fair value is the Price/Book ratio. Book value of RGTI's net assets is approximately $372 million as of the latest reportable date, meaning that the P/B ratio is above 20. Paying for a company 20x the price of its net assets does not look like a good deal. Moreover, the company continues burning cash, meaning that the book value is poised to deteriorate, which will result in an expanding P/B ratio.

Of course, the company's cash balance might grow if RGTI continues aggressively issuing more shares as they did in 2024-2025. However, the company's number of outstanding shares has more than doubled since early 2024, meaning that shareholders' value per share is diluting. For example, RGTI's book value per share was $1.2 as of the end of FY2022. As of the latest reportable date, it was $1.14. Meanwhile, RGTI's share price has grown by more than 3,400% since late December 2022.

If we look at the long-term consensus revenue revision trend above, we can see that commercialization timing forecasts were much more optimistic in December 2022 than they are now. The screenshot suggests that previously analysts forecasted $140 million in sales for FY2025, while RGTI is not expected to achieve this level even if we combine current revenue forecasts for three years between FY2025 and FY2027. Moreover, back in December 2022, analysts forecasted that RGTI will generate positive EPS in 2025. Therefore, RGTI's crazy share price rally since late 2022 was fueled by pure speculation.

And RGTI keeps disappointing. The company consistently delivers double earnings misses, and the last time it delivered a positive revenue surprise was almost two years ago. Please do not get me wrong here. I am aware that for a young company, inconsistent earnings surprise record is normal. And I am not saying that the management is unable to achieve its goals or something like that. On the contrary, I admire people who dedicate their lives to science and disruptive technologies. However, RGTI's poor earnings surprise record together with substantially deteriorated revenue and EPS forecasts since late 2022, underscores how difficult it is to achieve commercialization in this domain. Therefore, risks and uncertainties surrounding investing in RGTI are extremely high, meaning that its current valuation is not justifiable.

Reasons to invest in RGTI

Headlines often affect share prices more than fundamentals over the short term. The largest tech companies are also investing billions in developing quantum computing technologies, meaning that their CEOs sometimes share their opinion on this topic. Therefore, when Jensen Huang or Sundar Pichai say that they believe that quantum computing has great potential, it might trigger strong rallies for all popular stocks with quantum computing exposure. We saw it last summer when Jensen Huang said that quantum computing it nearing its "inflection point". Therefore, RGTI might see notable sentiment-driven rallies despite questionable fundamentals.

Besides potential big headlines, there might be real developments that can lead to RGTI's share price spike. Last year we saw a quite new phenomenon when Meta invested almost $15 billion in Scale AI to recruit the CEO of this company, Alexandr Wang, to join their AI "superintelligence" team. While I think that RGTI's $8+ billion market cap screams overvaluation, this amount is not that big in the modern world in absolute terms. For example, if Meta or Amazon decide that they need quantum computing business, their balance sheets are strong enough to afford them to buy a company worth $10 billion or $20 billion. Yes, RGTI appears to be still quite far from commercialization. However, the company has been working on this technology since 2013, and the team certainly has deep expertise in this domain. Therefore, a scenario where one of the tech giants acquires RGTI does not look impossible.

Last but not least, Wall Street analysts (on average) do not agree with me as they believe that RGTI is almost 50% undervalued. The average target price is $38, meaning that prominent analysts believe RGTI has a compelling upside potential. Several large Wall Street names like Wedbush, Jefferies, and Mizuho initiated coverage of quantum computing stocks with quite optimistic outlooks. Optimistic stance of such reputable Wall Street analysts might be a solid share price tailwind for RGTI. Rigetti's closest peers that recently received positive coverage from the above mentioned analysts are IonQ (IONQ), D-Wave Quantum (QBTS), and Quantum Computing (QUBT).

Valuations of all four quantum computing stocks are sky-high, which we can see from the comparison in the above table. And RGTI is among the most generously valued ones across almost all available metrics. All four companies are in very early stages of development, but IONQ appears to be the most mature and commercially advanced one with almost $80 million in TTM sales. This company uses trapped ions, which are charged atoms held by lasers.

Actually, all four companies are developing different technologies, which is good for RGTI since it means less competition overlapping. While IONQ makes bet on trapped ions, RGTI uses superconducting qubits. D-Wave uses another technology, quantum annealing. Finally, QUBT uses photonics, which is light instead of matter, a completely different approach. The fact that all four players use differentiated approaches might be the reason why Wall Street analysts are becoming increasingly bullish about these companies.

The verdict

Despite a 56% share price decline since my previous bearish call, I think that RGTI remains substantially overvalued. The stock is still more expensive at the moment than it was a few years ago by approximately 30 times while earnings revisions over the last couple of years suggest that commercialization has not become much closer. Therefore, RGTI's current multi-billion valuation appears to be unfairly high, which justifies a Strong Sell rating.

data.title: Range Resources: Around $500 Million In Projected 2026 Free Cash Flow
data.description:
Range Resources (RRC) is projected to generate close to $500 million in free cash flow in 2026 at current strip prices. The 2026 strip prices for natural gas, NGLs, and oil have all declined since I last looked at Range, so that reduces its estimated value from $41 per share to approximately $39.50 per share.

My long-term commodity prices remain unchanged, including $3.75 NYMEX natural gas and $25 NGLs (for Range). At those prices, Range may be able to generate close to $1 billion in free cash flow in 2027.

2026 Production Growth

Range expects to grow production significantly during the next couple of years. This includes going from around 2.23 Bcfe per day in 2025 production to approximately 2.4 Bcfe per day in 2026 production with a $650 million to $700 million capex budget. It also mentioned that it projects its reinvestment rate to be under 50% at $3.75 NYMEX natural gas, although current 2026 strip prices are below that.

I have been using $3.75 NYMEX natural gas as my long-term commodity price since that is a level where many producers like Range are comfortable increasing production levels to meet projected demand increases.

It appears that Range estimated it could achieve approximately $850 million in 2026 free cash flow in its October 2025 presentation. That was based on $3.75 NYMEX natural gas, a realized price of $25 for its NGLs, and $70 WTI oil, though. Current 2026 strip prices are below that for all those commodities.

At those commodity prices (such as $3.75 NYMEX natural gas), Range may be able to generate close to $1 billion in 2027 free cash flow.

Impact of NGL Prices

Around 31% of Range's production is liquids, but most of that is NGLs. Range's production was approximately 1.5% oil during the first three quarters of 2025. I expect close to 90% of Range's liquid revenues (and around 30% of its total revenues) to come from NGLs. Thus, NGL prices have a fairly significant impact on Range's financials.

Range's NGL barrels are 53% ethane, 27% propane, 8% butane, 4% isobutane, and 8% natural gasoline.

The current 2026 strip for all those NGLs is down from 2025 levels. Ethane and natural gasoline are down less than 10%, though, while propane, butane, and isobutane are down around 15%.

This leads to a projection that Range will realize approximately $21.50 per barrel for its NGLs in 2026 based on those strip prices.

Potential 2026 Results

I have modeled Range's potential 2026 results based on average production of 2.4 Bcfe (31% liquids). The current strip for 2026 involves approximately $60 WTI oil and $3.35 NYMEX natural gas.

Range has approximately 25% of its projected 2026 natural gas production hedged with a combination of swaps and three-way collars. These hedges have approximately $107 million in positive value at current strip prices.

This results in a projection of $2.928 billion in 2026 revenues for Range, including the value of its hedges.

Commodity

Units

Price Per Unit

$ Million

Natural Gas (Mcf)

602,250,000

Total

$2,928

This means that Range could generate $489 million in free cash flow in 2026 at current strip prices.

This is before cash income taxes, although cash income taxes should be minimal for Range in 2026 due to the weaker commodity pricing environment in 2026. Range mentioned a 5% effective tax rate in 2026 in its October 2025 presentation, but it was basing those calculations on $25 NGLs and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas.

Range should also see some savings from redeeming its $600 million in 8.25% unsecured notes due 2029. Range's credit facility had an effective interest rate of 6.4% at last report.

Notes on Valuation

I previously estimated Range's value at $41 per share but have slightly reduced that to $39.50 per share due to the reduced near-term free cash flow estimates. When I last looked at Range, the 2026 natural gas strip was around $3.90, around $0.55 higher than it is currently. This is partially mitigated by Range's hedges, but that still (along with NGL price changes) reduces its projected 2026 free cash flow by over $300 million.

I continue to use $3.75 NYMEX natural gas (along with $25 NGLs and $70 oil) as my long-term (after 2026) commodity prices.

Conclusion

Range's projected free cash flow in 2026 has fallen to around $500 million now due to weaker commodity prices. At $70 WTI oil and $3.75 NYMEX natural gas (along with $25 NGLs) instead, it could generate around $850 million in 2026 free cash flow and close to $1 billion in 2027 free cash flow.

The challenge for natural gas prices is that producers are well aware of the expectations for increased natural gas demand in future years and have been planning to increase their production accordingly given reasonable natural gas prices. I generally believe that $3.75 NYMEX natural gas is a reasonable price for natural gas and don't believe at this point in time that much higher natural gas prices can be sustained over a long period.

That being said, a long-term $3.75 NYMEX natural gas environment is fine for Range, and assuming that NGL prices also rebound to my longer-term expectations, Range's estimated value is around $39.50 per share.

data.title: AIPO: A Well Mixed ETF For The Ongoing AI And Energy Boom
data.description:
Here there is a novelty among ETFs that I must admit has caught my interest in the AI infrastructure segment: I’m talking about the Defiance AI & Power Infrastructure ETF (AIPO). A meeting point between technology and energy that seems to work well from a forward-looking perspective, at least in my opinion. And the market seems to think so as well, considering the very stretched valuations, and the strong momentum.

But since it is a young ETF, it is not easy to orient oneself on the price action, so the question I ask myself is: what is the best compass to move around AIPO? Well … if this is the question, let me give you an answer … but first.

What to know about AIPO

AIPO is an index tracking ETF linked to the (uncommon) segment of power demand and AI infrastructure. Launched in July 2025, it has little history, and therefore also low AUM, currently equal to $134.78M.

With an expense ratio of 0.69% and an average bid/ask spread of 0.13%, it replicates the MarketVector™ US Listed AI & Power Infrastructure Index.

The ETF is therefore a pure thematic beta, it is not designed with the objective of generating alpha, although its construction strategy seems to aim at that.

The strategy

To enter the index, a company must:

  • be US-listed
  • generate ≥50% of revenues from at least one of the 4 sub-themes

And here an interesting question comes into play: what are the official sub-themes? I’ll give you a fairly exhaustive list extracted from the prospectus:

  1. Power generation & grid equipment (50% of index weight)
  2. Construction & engineering (15%)
  3. Electric utilities & power producers (15%)
  4. Data centers & AI hardware (20%)

It is therefore immediately noticeable that the selection leans toward sectors not so close to technology, unlike typical AI funds. But let’s take a closer look right away …

Portfolio structure

The fund has about ~59 holdings, where the top 5 holdings account for ≈ 34% of NAV. This clearly indicates high concentration, so let’s immediately take a look at the main holdings:

A first aspect that I notice, and that I personally appreciate, is that there is no AI software company in the top 5 positions. The stocks in question are:

  • Pure industrials
  • Power & grid infrastructure
  • Electrical equipment
  • Strategic energy supply (uranium)

Then, among the subsequent holdings, there are also some companies linked to AI that are more commonly known (NVDA and AVGO). Two holdings that, in any case, barely contaminate the core of the ETF (less than 8% of market cap).

All the other companies depend on infrastructure spending for AI or, more or less, on the energy segment. AIPO is therefore implicitly betting on the fact that AI will impact energy consumption, and that there will be a need for increasingly advanced infrastructure and, if I may add, that this structure will in some way integrate nuclear power. In the end, the presence of Cameco at 5.8% and Constellation Energy Corp (3.90%) together weighs more than the big tech NVDA and AVGO within AIPO.

What drives it?

From the prospectus, in my view, it emerges quite clearly that the fund is much closer to an “AI Capex & Energy ETF” than to a pure Nasdaq-style AI fund. This means that (in theory) it benefits not from AI adoption, but from AI construction; and this should be confirmed by the fact that about 50% of the index is linked to power & grid equipment, a theme very close to me. In practice?

An interesting comparison, in my opinion, comes from an ETF that I personally appreciate a lot on AI in the traditional sense: AIQ. And likewise compared to an ETF that I personally selected among those in the grid infrastructure category: GRID.

AIPO is, in my opinion, somewhat a meeting point between these two solutions, but it also introduces external components, as we have specified for nuclear energy (a macro theme on which I am bullish anyway).

So, even though there is little historical data behind AIPO, in my view it makes sense to expect performance (and risk) behavior that is linear relative to these two solutions (which are more extreme in this sense).

Valuations

The point to reflect on is another one. The growth prospects of the sector are promising; precisely for this reason, the market today prices in extreme valuations. Specifically, I invite you to reflect on some metrics. The first classic ones:

  • the equally weighted average forward P/E (the so-called “rough average”) of the top 10 AIPO holdings exceeds 50x

  • The PEG exceeds 2x (again, rough average)

Even when moving to valuation metrics closer to CAPEX-heavy segments, such as, in my humble opinion, EV/EBITDA and Price to Book (two metrics I feel very confident with), the top 10 holdings still express extreme average values.

My opinion

Personally, I remain fairly bullish on all three macro themes that AIPO seems to touch through its holdings. Starting from the technology theme, which is relatively less present in AIPO, I remain positive nonetheless: guidance for US technology companies signals positivity in expected revenues and earnings.

This element reflects the investments (CAPEX) that were widely discussed in 2025. In turn, this is something from which (in my opinion) the entire underlying infrastructure benefits, which in this case represents the backbone of AIPO’s holdings distribution, an element that I happened to discuss precisely when taking a deeper look at GRID. Finally, energy demand is a direct consequence, and personally I see nuclear power as a direct outcome, an element that instead I explored when discussing NLR.

In all three cases, however, the issue to contend with is always the same: valuations. They currently price in particularly high growth expectations across all three segments.

Risk

And the valuation issue, in my view, exposes investors to an underestimated risk. I want to stress that AIPO does not have a defensive holding distribution in the true sense of the word. Although it mainly includes industrials and utilities, it remains a highly cyclical segment and very closely tied to the technology market: if data centers slow down, AIPO suffers immediately.

Why? Because the market necessarily has to reprice expectations, and on AIPO this repricing mechanism could be more violent than expected, considering the extremely stretched valuations.

Conclusion

I appreciate AIPO and the segments it manages to cover overall. Just as the market seems to price in, I also expect an expansion of this sector in the future.
